Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd just... not feel like it. I have about 25 hours a week theoretically dedicated to studying, but a lot of that time I'd be staring at my laptop feeling bored or anxious about everything I needed to do. With nine online courses, I spread myself thin and never really committed to a solid schedule for any of them.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything, then panic when a deadline approached and try to cram everything in. It wasn't intentional—I just couldn't seem to motivate myself until the pressure became unbearable. The anxiety would build up, and then I'd have these frantic study sessions that left me exhausted.
Q3: Usually it was guilt or panic that got me to log in. I'd get an email notification about a deadline or realize I hadn't checked anything in days. Sometimes I'd log in just to make myself feel like I was doing something productive, even if I ended up just clicking around without really engaging. Boredom would sometimes drive me there too, but then I'd get bored with the content itself.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. I never got into a rhythm where logging in felt natural or automatic. There were maybe a couple of weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit, but it never stuck. The platform just felt like another obligation hanging over my head rather than something integrated into my daily life.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful for me since I learn better by listening. I could put them on and absorb information without having to read through walls of text. I watched quite a bit of video content—over two hours worth—because it felt less demanding than other activities. At least with audio, I could feel like I was learning even when my motivation was low.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums. I posted maybe once the entire course. The whole idea of performing engagement publicly just felt exhausting and pointless to me. I also delayed most assignments until the last possible moment, which is why my completion rate is so low. Reading materials I'd skim or skip entirely if they seemed dense.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was directly tied to passing the course or if it genuinely interested me, which was rare. If I could see an immediate connection between doing something and my grade, I'd consider it. But anything that felt like busywork or extra fluff? I just couldn't bring myself to care enough to engage with it.
Q8: Optional activities might as well not exist for me. If something isn't required, my brain immediately categorizes it as 'not my problem.' Required activities at least had the threat of consequences attached, which gave me some external motivation. Without that pressure, I just couldn't manufacture the internal drive to do extra work.
Q9: Yes, definitely. I'd download lecture slides or PDFs and study from those on my tablet away from the LMS. I also used podcasts and YouTube videos on related topics when I found the course materials boring. Sometimes I'd screenshot important information and review it on my phone during commutes. A lot of my actual learning happened outside that platform.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going over downloaded materials or listening to supplementary audio content I found elsewhere. I'd also talk through concepts out loud to myself—that's how I process things best as an auditory learner. None of that shows up anywhere in the system. My exam score reflects learning that definitely didn't happen through tracked LMS activity.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, maybe download the slides, and then never return to that module in the LMS again. I'd review those materials multiple times offline, but the system would only show that single initial access. It probably looked like I barely engaged when I actually spent decent time with certain content.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety was through the roof, and I just felt paralyzed by how much I had to do across all nine courses. I'd sit down to study and feel this overwhelming boredom mixed with dread. The CBT sessions helped a bit with managing the anxiety, but the underlying disconnection from the coursework remained.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was stressed or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS and just... freeze. I'd click through things without absorbing anything. Fatigue made everything feel impossible—I have other responsibilities outside school, and balancing everything left me depleted. The LMS became something I associated with stress rather than learning.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have the course materials open while my mind was completely elsewhere, thinking about my anxiety or what else I needed to do. I'd watch videos at 2x speed just to get through them without actually processing the content. It was engagement theater—looking busy without actually being present.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—like this huge wall I couldn't climb. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would become next week. By the time I finally started, I'd rush through and submit something mediocre. My 32% completion rate tells that story pretty clearly.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me engage. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely. But they also caused these intense stress spikes where I'd cram everything at the last minute. My response time averaging over 17 hours shows how I'd wait until pressure forced my hand. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only system that worked for me.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss. Sometimes getting a decent grade would give me a brief motivational boost, but it never lasted. Negative feedback just added to my anxiety without really changing my behavior. I think I'd already mentally checked out enough that feedback felt like it was happening to someone else.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so many modules and activities. I never felt like I had a clear path through everything. The flexibility that was probably meant to help actually made it easier for me to drift and procrastinate. I needed more structure, not less, but I also probably wouldn't have followed it anyway.
Q19: The video content being available was helpful for my learning style. But the discussion requirements felt forced and pointless—I did the bare minimum there. The sheer volume of content across my courses made everything feel like a chore. Having clearer priorities about what actually mattered would have helped me focus my limited energy.
Q20: That makes total sense to me. Someone might be logging in, clicking around, watching videos, but never actually completing the final step of submitting work. The anxiety of turning something in and being judged is different from just passively consuming content. I've definitely been that person—active in the system but paralyzed when it came to producing anything.
Q21: That's somewhat me. My exam score was decent even though my LMS activity was pretty low. I learned a lot through offline methods and my own auditory processing of materials. The LMS doesn't capture studying from downloaded content, listening to external resources, or just thinking through concepts. Performance and platform activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click, how long they spend. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if someone is genuinely learning or just going through the motions. They definitely can't capture offline studying, mental state, or whether the time spent was actually productive.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to the material. It's not just showing up or clicking through requirements—it's being mentally present and curious. Honestly, I wasn't really engaged this semester. I was going through the motions, managing my anxiety, and trying to pass. That's not the same thing.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I struggled with anxiety and boredom throughout this course. It can't capture the hours I spent with downloaded materials or audio resources. It doesn't reflect the internal battle between wanting to succeed and feeling completely disconnected from everything. My engagement was complicated in ways that clicks and timestamps can't measure.
